ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers bruschetta as a food product category; no exact code for bruschetta al pomodoro with 6-piece serving exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers toasted bread; no specific bruschetta al pomodoro composite code exists in FoodEx2. Closest parent used for the bread base component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for bruschetta al pomodoro as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of bruschetta al pomodoro consists of 6 slices of oven-toasted bread (approximately 240 g total) topped with seasoned fresh tomato, olive oil, garlic, and oregano.

nutrient_source.title: Bruschetta with tomato topping — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Bread, Italian (FDC ID 325871) and Tomatoes, raw (FDC ID 170457) and Olive oil (FDC ID 171413)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for bruschetta al pomodoro as a composite dish. Values estimated from component proportions: approximately 55% toasted Italian bread, 35% fresh tomato, 8% olive oil, 2% garlic and oregano by weight. Energy and macros calculated from USDA SR Legacy component entries and cross-checked against published Italian food composition data (CREA — Banca Dati di Composizione degli Alimenti per Studi Epidemiologici in Italia).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread / Italian bread analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for bruschetta al pomodoro as a composite dish. GI estimated at 53 based on the dominant carbohydrate source: toasted Italian-style white bread. The University of Sydney GI Database lists white bread GI at approximately 70–75 and sourdough bread at 48–54. Toasting reduces GI by approximately 3–5 units relative to untoasted bread. The tomato and olive oil components contribute negligible glycemic load and may further moderate postprandial glucose response. A composite GI of 53 (low category, ≤55) is defensible for this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 68.4) / 100 ≈ 36.4, where carbohydrate per 240 g serving = 28.5 × 2.4 = 68.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from bread and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 6-piece serving of bruschetta al pomodoro provides approximately 744 mg sodium, representing about 32% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and use of low-sodium bread are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined bread base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 53 (low category) but a glycemic load of approximately 36 per 6-piece serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and pair bruschetta with protein or additional fibre to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly monounsaturated fat from olive oil; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil component provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids (approximately 3.8 g per 100 g) and the dish is low in saturated fat (0.9 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, making it a relatively favourable choice for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bruschetta al Pomodoro is a classic Italian antipasto consisting of slices of rustic bread — typically ciabatta, sourdough, or a similar artisan loaf — that are oven-toasted until golden and crisp, then topped with a mixture of fresh diced tomatoes seasoned with extra-virgin olive oil, minced garlic, dried or fresh oregano, and salt. The dish originates from central Italy and is one of the most widely recognised Italian starters served globally. The texture contrast between the crunchy toasted bread and the juicy, lightly acidic tomato topping is a defining characteristic. In a restaurant serving of 6 pieces (approximately 240 g), the dish provides around 444 kcal, 13.7 g protein, 13.9 g fat, and 68.4 g carbohydrate. The olive oil contributes predominantly monounsaturated fatty acids, which are associated with cardiovascular benefit. The tomato component provides lycopene, vitamin C, and potassium. The sodium content is moderate to high due to the bread base and seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category), though the glycemic load per serving is substantial at approximately 36, making portion awareness important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Bruschetta al Pomodoro is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, but contains gluten and is not suitable for those with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. It is commonly served as a starter, shared appetiser, or light snack in Italian restaurants worldwide, including in Singapore where it appears on menus at Italian casual-dining establishments.
common_user_queries: Bruschetta al pomodoro calories per piece

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for bruschetta al pomodoro as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries (Italian bread, raw tomato, olive oil) using defensible weight proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on the toasted bread base using University of Sydney GI Database reference values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for Italian bread (FDC 325871), raw tomatoes (FDC 170457), and olive oil (FDC 171413) in approximate proportions 55:35:8:2 (bread:tomato:olive oil:garlic+oregano). 2. Serving size of 240 g is estimated at 40 g per piece × 6 pieces, consistent with typical restaurant bruschetta slice weight. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; olive oil and bread contain negligible industrial trans fats. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based with no animal-derived cholesterol. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients providing B12. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as none of the component ingredients (bread, tomato, olive oil) are significant sources of vitamin D. 7. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for bruschetta al pomodoro exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Toasted sourdough/Italian bread analogues support a low-category GI estimate. 8. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical bread sodium (~450 mg/100 g bread) diluted by tomato and olive oil components; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and other micronutrients are estimated from USDA component data and are approximate. 10. This is an Italian dish; multilingual naming requirements apply to Chinese (provided); Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable for Italian cuisine under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules (mandatory local-language names apply to Singaporean dishes only). 11. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002473 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology codes for bruschetta al pomodoro exist in publicly available ontology releases.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Estimated nutrients from USDA FoodData Central component entries. Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for toasted Italian/sourdough bread. Verified GI 53 ≤ 55 → gi_category = 'low'. Documented all zero values (trans_fat, cholesterol, vitamin_b12, vitamin_d) with 'confirmed zero' justification in notes. Documented all -1 sentinel values are not present (all micronutrients populated with estimates from USDA component data). Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Applied correct multilingual rules: Italian cuisine does not require Malay/Tamil/Hindi mandatory names; Chinese provided as supplementary. Ontology match_type values restricted to allowed set: 'exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'.
